Sure, focusing on geometry: Wide lenses (short focal lengths): - Straight lines remain straight if the lens has low distortion, but the projection causes objects near the edges to appear stretched because you’re mapping a wide field of view onto a flat frame. - Parallel lines can appear to diverge dramatically when the camera is close and tilted (converging verticals). - Circles near edges become ellipses due to perspective and projection. Long lenses (long focal lengths): - Less stretching at edges because the field of view is narrower; objects maintain more consistent shape across the frame. - Space appears flattened; depth cues from size change are reduced because the camera is farther away for similar framing. Also, lens distortion (barrel/pincushion) adds on top of these projection effects. A well-corrected wide prime can have straight lines, but you’ll still see edge stretching due to perspective if you’re close.
Is edge stretching purely perspective, or lens?

Mostly perspective/projection, not lens distortion. - Perspective/projection stretching happens because with a wide field of view, objects at the edge are seen at a more oblique angle relative to the camera’s axis, so their projected shapes stretch. This is inherent to rectilinear projection. - Lens distortion bends straight lines (barrel/pincushion). That’s an optical imperfection (or design tradeoff), separate from perspective. A fisheye lens intentionally uses a different projection that keeps more uniform area mapping but curves straight lines. A rectilinear wide lens keeps lines straight but stretches edges.
How do cinematographers avoid making people look stretched at the edges?

Common strategies: - Keep faces and bodies closer to the center of frame on wide lenses. - Don’t go too wide for close-ups unless you want the effect. - Increase camera distance slightly and use a slightly longer lens. - Use wider lenses mainly for establishing or for shots where edge stretching isn’t on faces. - Compose with “safe zones” where you avoid placing important anatomy at extreme edges. Also, blocking helps: keep actors away from frame edges if you’re on a 18–24mm and close.
What about aspect ratio? Does that influence lens choice?

Yes, because aspect ratio changes composition and what focal lengths feel “right.” - Wider aspect ratios (2.39:1) encourage horizontal compositions. Cinematographers may choose focal lengths that allow lateral staging—often slightly wider lenses to include multiple subjects across the frame, or longer lenses to create layered horizontal bands. - Taller ratios (1.85:1, 16:9) can make wide lenses feel more expansive vertically, and you may choose different focal lengths to manage ceilings, headroom, and vertical lines. In anamorphic 2.39:1, the horizontal field of view is emphasized. Some DPs choose slightly longer anamorphic focal lengths for close-ups because the format already feels wide.
Is there a “best” focal length for close-ups?

No single best, but there are common preferences. On Super 35: close-ups often land around 50–100mm. On full frame: close-ups often land around 75–135mm. But it depends on: - Desired intimacy (closer wide vs distant long). - Actor comfort and performance. - Location constraints. - Lens set characteristics (some 75s look magical, some don’t). - Whether you want to feel the environment or isolate. Some iconic close-ups are on wider lenses precisely because they feel confrontational and present.
How do you think about “lens speed” in cinema? Is faster always better?

Faster (wider maximum aperture) is a tool, not automatically better. Pros of fast lenses: - Low-light capability. - Shallow DoF options. - Potentially unique wide-open character. Cons: - More challenging focus. - Wide-open aberrations can be undesirable. - Might encourage lighting that’s too dim or flat if used to avoid lighting rather than as a creative choice. - Very shallow DoF can become a stylistic crutch and can reduce spatial clarity. Many productions prefer lenses that look great at T2.8/T4 and use lighting to shape the scene, rather than living at T1.3 constantly.
You mentioned “spatial clarity.” Can shallow depth of field ever hurt storytelling?

Yes. If DoF is too shallow: - You lose geography and context; scenes can feel like floating faces. - Eye-lines and reactions can be harder to read if focus misses or if the wrong plane is sharp. - Editing can feel disjointed because each shot is isolated. - The audience may feel visually fatigued if everything is constantly blurred. Shallow DoF is powerful when used selectively: to direct attention, emphasize isolation, or heighten emotion. But deep focus can be equally cinematic and sometimes more effective for complex staging.
Deep focus reminds me of older films. Was that because of film limitations or style?

Both. Historically, deep focus was influenced by: - Lighting levels: older film stocks were slower (lower ISO), requiring more light, which often meant stopping down (smaller apertures) and thus more depth of field. - Lens design and format: certain formats and lenses encouraged deeper DoF. - Aesthetic choices: directors and DPs used deep focus for staging complexity, letting the audience choose where to look. Modern digital can shoot in low light, which makes shallow DoF easier, so the “default” can drift toward shallow. But deep focus remains a deliberate and often striking choice.
If I want to understand why a particular movie “looks” a certain way, what questions should I ask myself while watching?

A useful checklist: 1) Camera placement and movement - Is the camera close or distant? - Is it static, handheld, dolly, crane, stabilized? 2) Lens language - Does the film favor wide, normal, or long lenses? - Do close-ups feel intimate (close wide) or observational (distant long)? 3) Depth of field strategy - Mostly shallow, mostly deep, or varied by scene? - Is focus used to direct attention? 4) Light quality and direction - Soft vs hard, high-key vs low-key, motivated practicals vs stylized sources. 5) Contrast and color palette - Are blacks lifted or crushed? - Are highlights rolled off gently or punchy? - Are skin tones natural, warm, cool, stylized? 6) Texture - Grain/noise, halation, diffusion, sharpness. 7) Production design and wardrobe - Colors, patterns, reflective surfaces, set density. 8) Post and finish - Film emulation, LUT style, HDR vs SDR. Answering these helps you locate the causes of “look” more precisely than “film vs digital.”
This is a lot, but it’s exactly what I wanted. One last thing: if someone insists “film is always better,” how would you respond in a technical but fair way?

I’d separate “better” into measurable properties and aesthetic preference. Technically: - Modern digital can match or exceed film in resolution, low-light performance, and often dynamic range in shadows. - Film has distinct grain structure, halation, and highlight behavior that many find pleasing and that can be difficult to replicate perfectly. - Both can be mastered beautifully; both can look bad if mishandled. Aesthetically: - Film is a strong, coherent aesthetic package—its imperfections are often flattering and familiar. - Digital is more variable; it can look sterile or it can be crafted to look rich, depending on choices. Practically: - Film imposes discipline and a certain workflow; digital offers flexibility and efficiency. Those affect creative decisions, which affect the image. So “film is always better” is too broad. A more accurate statement might be: “Film has a specific rendering that I prefer for certain stories,” which is both true and useful.
Great. Actually, I lied—another last thing. If I wanted to train my eye, what exercises would you recommend?

A few exercises that work well: 1) Freeze-frame focal length study Pick a film and pause on shots. For each shot, write: - perceived camera distance (close/medium/far) - field of view (wide/normal/long) - background scale cues - edge stretching cues Then check behind-the-scenes stills if available to see if your intuition matches. 2) Parallax observation Watch scenes with dolly moves. Notice how foreground and background separate. Strong separation suggests wider lens/closer camera. 3) Shot-reverse-shot comparison In dialogue scenes, compare the two singles: - Are they on similar focal lengths? - Does one character get a wider lens/closer camera (more distortion) to imply tension? - Does the lensing change after a story beat? 4) Replicate with a camera (even a phone) Use a phone’s main camera (wide) and then step back and zoom/crop to mimic a longer lens. Compare perspective and background scale. This teaches the camera-position principle fast. 5) Aspect ratio and composition Watch the same type of scene in different aspect ratios and note how lens choices and staging change. Over time, you’ll start “feeling” the lensing strategy as part of the storytelling grammar rather than as isolated technical trivia.
That’s perfect. I’m going to try the phone exercise first.
